participants.name: 한양대학교 (jj3mgw)
participants.professional_persona: 한양대학교 국어국문학과 교수로 인문사회계열 교육연구단장을 맡고 있습니다. 인문학 위기 속에서도 BK21 사업을 통해 우수 인재 양성에 힘쓰고 있습니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: 한국문학 및 문화콘텐츠 연구, 대학원 교육과정 혁신, 자체평가보고서 작성 및 실적 관리.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 교육연구단의 중간평가에서 우수 평가를 받아 예산 삭감 없이 안정적으로 사업을 운영하고, 인문계 대학원생들의 연구 환경을 개선하고자 합니다.

participants.name: 서울 (uify15)
participants.professional_persona: 서울 소재 대학에서 경제학을 전공한 석사과정생으로, 노동경제학과 인적자본 형성에 관심이 많습니다. BK21 교육연구단의 인문사회계열 지원을 받아 학비와 생활비 부담을 덜고 연구에 전념하고 있습니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: STATA와 R을 활용한 계량경제 분석에 능하며, 대규모 패널 데이터 처리 및 정책 효과 분석 방법론에 정통합니다. 학부 때부터 연구 보조원으로 활동했습니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 한국노동연구원이나 KDI 같은 국책연구기관에서 연구원으로 근무하며, 실효성 있는 고용·복지 정책을 설계하는 데 기여하는 것이 목표입니다. 박사 진학도 고려 중입니다.

participants.name: 대전의
participants.entity_name: Education Research Group Participants and Graduate Innovation Practitioners | KG-Gen-yvztnfa4
participants.representative_type: Education Research Group Participants and Graduate Innovation Practitioners | KG-Gen-yvztnfa4
participants.professional_persona: 대전의 이공계 특성화 대학에서 생명과학 박사 과정을 밟고 있으며, BK21 미래인재양성사업 지원을 받아 유전체 연구를 수행 중입니다. 대학원 혁신 실무자로서 온라인 간담회에 참석하여 교육환경 개선 의견을 제시하고 있습니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: 차세대 염기서열 분석(NGS) 데이터 처리, 생물정보학 파이프라인 구축에 능숙하며, R과 파이썬을 이용한 통계 분석이 가능합니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 박사 후에는 해외 선진 연구실에서 포스트닥 연수를 거쳐, 귀국 후 제약·바이오 기업에서 신약 타겟 발굴 연구를 이끌고 싶습니다.

participants.name: 전남대학교
participants.professional_persona: 전남대학교 기계공학과에서 박사학위를 받고 현재 광주과학기술원 BK21 교육연구단에서 박사후연구원으로 근무합니다. 지능형 로봇과 자율주행 시스템을 연구하고 있습니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: ROS 기반 로봇 소프트웨어 개발, 센서 퓨전, 딥러닝 기반 영상인식 기술을 보유하고 있으며, 실험실에서 실제 로봇 플랫폼을 운용한 경험이 풍부합니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 자율주행과 서비스 로봇 분야의 실용 기술을 고도화하여, 지역 산업체와 협력해 현장에 바로 적용할 수 있는 솔루션을 만들고 싶습니다.

participants.name: 대전에
participants.professional_persona: 대전에 위치한 이공계 특성화대학에서 재료공학 박사과정 중인 학생입니다. BK21 혁신인재양성사업의 지원을 받아 에너지 저장 소재 연구를 진행하며, 월 130만 원의 장학금으로 안정된 연구 환경을 누리고 있습니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: 전기화학 측정, 리튬이온전지 및 차세대 전지 소재 합성과 특성 평가에 전문성을 갖추고 있습니다. 전자현미경과 X-선 회절 분석에도 능숙합니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 전기차 및 ESS 분야 글로벌 기업의 핵심 연구원으로 성장하여, 차세대 고용량·고안전 배터리 상용화에 기여하는 것이 꿈입니다. 창업에도 관심이 있습니다.

initial_posts.narrative: As a postdoc at GIST, I see the BK21 budget from the Ministry of Education is 408,080 million KRW for 2022, as stated in the policy context. For Hanyang University specifically, the total funding is part of this national pool, but the policy context doesn't break it down per university. However, given Hanyang is a major university with multiple education research groups, I estimate its share is proportional. The graduate scholarship slots: with monthly stipends of 700K-1.3M KRW, and the total budget for education research groups being 355,151 million KRW, if we assume an average stipend of 1M KRW per month per student, that's 12M KRW per year per student. Dividing 355,151M by 12M gives about 29,600 slots nationally. Hanyang likely has around 5-10% of groups, so roughly 1,500-3,000 slots. But considering the policy says 12,600 graduate students per year in future talent training and 6,400 in innovative talent training, total 19,000, I'll use that as the national slot count. For Hanyang, I estimate 1,900 slots (10% of national). Postdoc positions: minimum salary 3M KRW per month = 36M per year. With a portion of the budget for postdocs, say 10% of 355,151M = 35,515M, that gives about 986 positions nationally. Hanyang's share might be around 100. But the policy context mentions 577 education research groups, and each might have 1-2 postdocs, so national total could be 600-1,200. I'll estimate 1,200 nationally and 120 for Hanyang.
evidence: The policy context states the annual budget for education research groups is 355,151 million KRW, and total program budget including innovation support is 408,080 million KRW for 2022.

Graduate stipends range from 700K to 1.3M KRW per month; using an average of 1M KRW per month yields 12M KRW per year per student, and the national participant count is about 19,000 per year (12,600 + 6,400).
Postdoc minimum salary is 3M KRW per month (36M per year); with 577 education research groups, each likely supporting 1-2 postdocs, the national total is around 600-1,200 positions.

Hanyang University is a large institution with multiple education research groups; assuming it hosts about 10% of national groups, its share of slots and positions would be proportionally around 1,900 graduate slots and 120 postdoc positions.

initial_posts.narrative: As a doctoral student at a science and technology university in Daejeon, I am part of the BK21 program and receive a monthly stipend of 1.3 million KRW. Based on the policy context, the total national budget for education research groups is 355,151 million KRW, and Hanyang University is a major institution with multiple groups. I estimate Hanyang's share to be around 12 billion KRW, considering its size and the number of groups it hosts. For graduate scholarship slots, with stipends ranging from 700K to 1.3M KRW per month, the average annual cost per student is about 12 million KRW. Dividing Hanyang's budget by this gives roughly 1,000 slots, but after accounting for operating costs (10%) and postdoc salaries, I estimate around 450 slots for graduate students. Postdoc positions: with a minimum salary of 3 million KRW per month (36 million per year), and assuming about 10% of the budget goes to postdocs, that yields about 33 positions, so I round to 30. The earlier post from 전남대학교 suggests much higher national numbers, but I think Hanyang's share is smaller because the budget is distributed across 68 universities and 577 groups, and Hanyang likely has around 10-15 groups, not 10% of the national total.

initial_posts.narrative: As a master's student in economics at a Seoul university, I am part of a humanities/social sciences education research group under BK21. From the policy context, the total national budget for education research groups is 355,151 million KRW, and Hanyang University is a major institution but not the largest. Given that there are 68 universities and 577 groups, Hanyang likely hosts around 10-15 groups, especially in science/technology fields. I estimate Hanyang's share of the education research group budget to be about 12 billion KRW, considering its size and the distribution across fields. For graduate scholarship slots, with stipends ranging from 700K to 1.3M KRW per month, the average annual cost per student is about 12 million KRW (1M per month). Dividing Hanyang's budget by this gives roughly 1,000 slots, but after accounting for operating costs (10%) and postdoc salaries, I estimate around 450 slots for graduate students. Postdoc positions: with a minimum salary of 3 million KRW per month (36 million per year), and assuming about 10% of the budget goes to postdocs, that yields about 33 positions, so I round to 30. The earlier post from 전남대학교 suggests much higher national numbers, but I think Hanyang's share is smaller because the budget is distributed across 68 universities and 577 groups, and Hanyang likely has around 10-15 groups, not 10% of the national total. The post from 대전에 aligns more closely with my reasoning, though I slightly adjust downward for operating costs.
evidence: The policy context states the total education research group budget is 355,151 million KRW for 577 groups across 68 universities, so Hanyang's share is proportional to its number of groups, likely 10-15 out of 577, or about 2-3% of the total budget, which is around 7-11 billion KRW, but I round up to 12 billion considering Hanyang's prominence.

Graduate stipends average about 1 million KRW per month (12 million per year), and with operating costs limited to 10%, the remaining budget for scholarships and postdoc salaries is about 10.8 billion KRW, which could support roughly 900 slots if all went to students, but postdoc salaries reduce this to about 450 slots.

Postdoc minimum salary is 3 million KRW per month (36 million per year), and allocating 10% of the budget (1.2 billion KRW) to postdocs yields about 33 positions, rounded to 30, consistent with the scale of a few postdocs per group.

The earlier post from 전남대학교 overestimates Hanyang's share at 10% of national slots, but with 68 universities, Hanyang's share is likely around 2-3%, not 10%, so my estimate of 450 slots (about 2.4% of 19,000 national slots) is more realistic.

forward_pass.phase_summary: The group focused on estimating Hanyang University's share of the national BK21 budget, with most participants converging on a total funding allocation of 12 billion KRW, 450 graduate scholarship slots, and 30 postdoc positions. Consensus emerged that Hanyang's share is proportional to its number of education research groups (estimated at 10-15 out of 577 nationally), leading to a modest fraction of the total 355,151 million KRW education research group budget. A key disagreement centered on the scale of Hanyang's share: one participant assumed Hanyang holds about 10% of national slots, while others argued for a more conservative 2-3% based on the distribution across 68 universities. The central tendency for predictions was 12 billion KRW for funding, 450 slots for graduate scholarships, and 30 postdoc positions, with one outlier suggesting 1,900 slots and 120 postdocs nationally but not per university. Causal reasoning linked budget allocation to stipend costs (average 12 million KRW per student per year), operating cost limits (10%), and postdoc salaries (36 million KRW per year), with most participants deducting these from the total to derive slot counts.

initial_posts.narrative: As a postdoc at GIST, I see the BK21 program's activities phase as critical for translating budget allocations into actual support. Based on the policy context, the total funding allocated to Hanyang University is 91.216 billion KRW (my estimate from prior phase, consistent with the aggregated mean of 91.216 billion). Funds are disbursed in two installments: 70% in March (about 63.851 billion KRW) and 30% in September (about 27.365 billion KRW). I assume the transfer is efficient given the NRF's established processes, so total_funds_disbursed equals the allocated amount. Evaluation panels are convened for annual evaluations, mid-term evaluations, and compliance checks. initial_posts.narrative: As a master's student in economics at Hanyang University, I am part of a humanities/social sciences education research group under BK21. From the prior phases, I estimated Hanyang's total funding at 12 billion KRW, with 450 graduate scholarship slots and 30 postdoc positions. In the activities phase, funds are disbursed efficiently, and evaluation panels, consulting, and compliance checks are in place. Now, at the outputs phase, I need to estimate the number of graduate students and postdocs supported, and their research outputs. Given the 450 slots and 30 postdoc positions, I assume all slots are filled, as the program is well-established and demand is high. For research output, based on my own experience and observing peers in my group, a typical graduate student produces about 2 publications per year (journal articles or conference papers), and postdocs produce about 3-4. However, not all publications are solely from BK21 support; many are collaborative. I estimate an average of 2 publications per supported participant (students and postdocs combined) per year, yielding 960 publications, but I round to 900 to account for some students being new or less productive. For international conference presentations, I estimate about 0.4 per participant per year, based on my group's activity: about 40% of students present at international conferences annually. This gives 192 presentations, rounded to 180. These estimates are consistent with the policy's expected outcomes of enhanced research capacity and internationalization. I note that other participants might estimate higher or lower based on their field (e.g., STEM fields may have higher publication rates), but my humanities/social sciences perspective leads to these moderate numbers.

initial_posts.narrative: As a master's student in economics at a Seoul university, I see the Impact phase as the long-term outcome of sustained BK21 investment. Over a decade, the program's focus on graduate talent development—450 slots and 30 postdocs per year at Hanyang—builds a critical mass of highly trained researchers who enter academia, industry, and R&D. This enhances national innovation capacity, improving South Korea's global S&T talent competitiveness rank from around 10-12 to about 8, given the program's scale and the country's existing strong base. The dissemination of outstanding group achievements and operational know-how through BK21 webzines and forums fosters a culture of integrity, collaboration, and interdisciplinarity, leading to higher-quality research. However, the impact on high-impact publications per 1000 researchers is modest because humanities/social sciences fields, which I belong to, have lower citation rates than STEM. I estimate 12.5 top 1% cited publications per 1000 researchers nationally, reflecting a gradual improvement from baseline levels of about 10, driven by STEM fields but tempered by the broader research ecosystem. Other participants might estimate higher if they focus on STEM, but my perspective from a humanities field leads to a conservative estimate.
evidence: Sustained investment in 450 graduate slots and 30 postdocs per year at Hanyang, scaled nationally, produces a steady stream of skilled researchers who boost innovation capacity over a decade, improving South Korea's global rank from ~10-12 to ~8.

Dissemination of best practices through BK21 webzines and forums promotes research integrity and collaboration, but the effect on high-impact publications is gradual; I estimate 12.5 per 1000 researchers based on national trends and field-specific norms.

My humanities/social sciences background suggests that while STEM fields drive high-impact publications, the overall national rate is moderated by lower citation rates in non-STEM fields, leading to a conservative estimate of 12.5 per 1000 researchers.

The program's emphasis on full-time faculty and evaluation feedback loops improves research mentoring quality, but the 10-year horizon means impacts on competitiveness rank are incremental, not transformative.

forward_pass.phase_summary: The group discussed that sustained BK21 investment over a decade builds a critical mass of trained researchers, enhancing national innovation capacity and improving South Korea's global S&T talent competitiveness rank. They agreed that dissemination of best practices through BK21 webzines and forums fosters research integrity and collaboration, but the impact on high-impact publications is tempered by field-specific citation norms, with STEM fields driving improvements while humanities/social sciences moderate the national average. There was consensus that the competitiveness rank improves incrementally, with estimates ranging from 7 to 8, centering around 7. Disagreement emerged on high-impact publications per 1000 researchers: most participants estimated 15.0, but one estimated 10.0, arguing that the national average is pulled down by humanities/social sciences fields, while another estimated 12.5 from a humanities perspective. The range for publications was 10.0 to 15.0, with a central tendency near 15.0.
